title: Das Bus

jokes.text: 300 cubits, give or take. - [Thunderclap] - Exactly 300!
jokes.setup: Noah casually estimates God's ark specifications as 'give or take'
jokes.punchline: God's immediate angry correction with thunderclap
jokes.explanation: Good quick comedy beat showing God as micromanaging. Economy is excellent with the immediate thunderclap correction.

dialogue.speaker: Noah
dialogue.line: So the ark is, what, 300 cubits long? Give or take?

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: 300 cubits EXACTLY. Not give or take.

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: *thunderclap*

jokes.text: - Even stink beetles? - Especially stink beetles.
jokes.setup: Noah questions if he needs to save even disgusting creatures
jokes.punchline: God's emphatic response about especially saving stink beetles
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective escalation joke. The 'especially' makes it absurd rather than just confirming.

dialogue.speaker: Noah
dialogue.line: Do I have to save the stink beetles?

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Especially the stink beetles!

jokes.text: Whoa, cool! God is so 'in your face.'
jokes.setup: Witnessing God's dramatic biblical interventions
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual, modern slang assessment of the divine
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart voice - applying contemporary teen slang to biblical majesty. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, God is like, totally awesome.

jokes.text: Yeah. He's my favorite fictional character.
jokes.setup: Discussion of God's dramatic presence
jokes.punchline: Lisa casually calling God her favorite 'fictional character'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Lisa line - her intellectual skepticism delivered with childlike innocence. Perfect character voice with subversive edge.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: God is my favorite fictional character.

jokes.text: At least let us watch till the midgets drown.
jokes.setup: Kids wanting to stay up to watch the biblical flood movie
jokes.punchline: Bart's casually morbid specific request
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart dark humor but relies somewhat on shock value. Still fits his character well.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Can we stay up and watch the biblical flood movie?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, it's a school night.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Dad, it's got tons of drowning!

jokes.text: David Versus Super-Goliath and Suddenly, Last Supper
jokes.setup: Lisa citing precedent for staying up to watch Troy McClure Bible movies
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous parody titles mixing biblical stories with movie clichés
jokes.explanation: Clever parody titles that satirize both biblical epics and Hollywood naming conventions. Good wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Remember when you stayed up all night to watch Troy McClure in 'The Ten Commandments: The Wrath of Khan'?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's not a real movie, Lisa.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What about 'Jonah: Part II' or 'The Book of Exodus: The Director's Cut'?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I remember 'Sodom and Gomorrah: The Musical.' That was terrible.

jokes.text: Go, Lise. Way to cite precedent.
jokes.setup: Lisa making a logical argument about staying up late
jokes.punchline: Bart's surprisingly sophisticated appreciation of her legal reasoning
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Bart's intelligence beneath his troublemaker exterior. Good sibling dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The sleep deprivation will impair your cognitive function, which will negatively impact your academic performance, creating a cumulative deficit that—

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. That's a pretty sweet argument. You should be a lawyer or something.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Thank you, Bart. So you'll go to bed?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No way. But I respect the hustle.

jokes.text: You've seen the movie. Now meet a real-life Noah. Only this Noah has been accused of killing two of every animal.
jokes.setup: Transition from biblical movie to news program about real Noah
jokes.punchline: Dark twist that this Noah is accused of animal murder
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy escalation and perfect structure. Classic Simpsons media satire.

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: We now return to our regularly scheduled program.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: This just in: Local man Noah Zuckerman has been arrested and charged with the murder of over 200 animals found dead in his basement.

jokes.text: Well, it wasn't wiped out in my house!
jokes.setup: Burns calling in sick claiming to have smallpox
jokes.punchline: His defensive response when challenged about an eradicated disease
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns character moment - his old-fashioned diseases and defensive reaction to modern reality.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I have smallpox.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: But smallpox has been eradicated.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, I'm very old.

jokes.text: And bone we will. [All Laughing]
jokes.setup: Teacher saying they need to 'bone up' on studies
jokes.punchline: Students laughing at the sexual innuendo
jokes.explanation: Predictable innuendo humor. Functional but not clever - relies on obvious double entendre.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: We really need to bone up on our studies.

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: Hehehehe.

jokes.text: Well, uh, I heard they sent a rocket to the sun once- at night.
jokes.setup: Ralph being asked about his nation's achievements
jokes.punchline: His hilariously illogical claim about a nighttime sun mission
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph Wiggum logic - completely absurd but delivered with innocent confidence. Highly quotable and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: We put a man on the sun. At night.

jokes.text: And there was that submarine with the screen doors.
jokes.setup: Ralph continuing his fictional national achievements
jokes.punchline: Another impossibly illogical invention
jokes.explanation: Another classic Ralph absurdity. Screen doors on submarines is perfectly illogical. Good follow-up to rocket joke.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I invented a device that lets you see sounds and hear colors.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I call it the soundoscope-o-vision.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: When I press this button, purple tastes like Tuesday.

jokes.text: Oh, grow up, Lise.
jokes.setup: Lisa complaining about the bone innuendo
jokes.punchline: Bart telling his more mature sister to 'grow up' about immature humor
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - the immature brother telling the mature sister to grow up. Solid character dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that joke was completely inappropriate with all those bone innuendos.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Geez, Lisa, grow up. It's just a joke.

jokes.text: the exports of Libya are numerous in amount. One thing they export is corn. Or, as the Indians call it, maize. Another famous Indian was Crazy Horse. In conclusion, Libya is a land of contrasts.
jokes.setup: Student trying to present about Libya's exports
jokes.punchline: Complete stream-of-consciousness nonsense ending with generic conclusion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating nonsense that satirizes both bad student presentations and generic travel writing. 'Land of contrasts' is particularly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Libya's main exports include oil, natural gas, and...

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: ...um, also bananas? No wait, they don't have bananas. But they have... sand? Yes, very fine sand. And camels. Do they export camels? I think so. Also carpets maybe. And those little lamps. You know the ones.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Anyway, in conclusion, Libya is a country with stuff, and that's why it's important.

jokes.text: Oh, I'd like to, but I'm afraid he has diplomatic immunity.
jokes.setup: Students asking teacher to stop another student's violence
jokes.punchline: Teacher citing diplomatic immunity in a Model UN club
jokes.explanation: Clever escalation of Model UN roleplay to absurd real-world consequences

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Mr. Johnson, you have to stop Ralph from hitting people!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: I'm afraid I can't do that.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Ralph has diplomatic immunity. He's the delegate from the Model UN club.

jokes.text: Point of 'odor.' Lisa stinks.
jokes.setup: Lisa making a serious point of order
jokes.punchline: Nelson's juvenile wordplay turning it into an insult
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective wordplay that fits Nelson's bullying character. Predictable but well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'd like to make a serious point of order.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: You said 'point of order'... order... ordure! Ha-ha!

jokes.text: Good luck, Ralphy. If your nose starts bleeding, it means you're picking it too much.
jokes.setup: Father giving son advice before Model UN trip
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate and obvious medical advice
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Chief Wiggum's dim parenting through practical advice

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now son, you're going to the Model UN, so I want to give you some advice. If you get a headache, just rub some butter on it. If your stomach hurts, drink some motor oil. And if you start bleeding from your ears, that's just your brain working too hard—don't worry about it.

jokes.text: Or not enough.
jokes.setup: Warning Ralph about nosebleeds from picking nose too much
jokes.punchline: Ned's contradictory medical advice
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan contradiction. Ned's well-meaning but illogical advice delivered matter-of-factly.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, Ralph, if you keep picking your nose like that, you're gonna get a nosebleed.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: But Mr. Flanders, you always tell me to pick my nose.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but not so hard, boy-diddly-boy. You gotta be gentle-diddly-entl— Oh, what's the use? Just don't do it at all!

jokes.text: Remember, Otto. We're trusting you with our greatest natural resource: the school bus.
jokes.setup: Skinner making a solemn speech about trust and precious resources
jokes.punchline: The 'resource' is the bus, not the children
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection that reveals Skinner's priorities - bureaucracy over children

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Children are our most precious resource. We must treat them with care and respect, nurturing their minds and protecting their futures.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: That's why I'm asking you all to take extra care of the school bus. It cost the district a lot of money.

jokes.text: Flancrest Enterprises is my home business. You liar. You don't have a home business. Why would you make up a lie like that? No. It's true. Maude and I sell religious hook rugs over the Internet.
jokes.setup: Ned caught with mysterious mail, Homer accusing him of lying
jokes.punchline: Ned's oddly specific and perfectly Flanders-esque business
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-specific business idea. Religious hook rugs over the Internet is exactly what Ned would do in 1998.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ned, what's all this mail about? You're hiding something!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, if you must know, I've started a little business. It's called 'Flanders' Lefty Loosey-Righty Tighty Fastener Emporium and Notary Public.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A what now?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: It's a one-stop shop for left-handed fasteners and notarization services. Got the idea during a church potluck. Perfectly legal, all above board, diddly-doodly!

jokes.text: You liar. You don't have a home business. Why would you make up a lie like that?
jokes.setup: Ned revealing he has a home business
jokes.punchline: Homer's indignant reaction to what seems impossible to him
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Homer's jealousy and disbelief

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A home business? But you're always at the Leftorium!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, the Leftorium IS my home business.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, so you work FROM home? But then where do you go during the day?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I just told you, Homer. I go to work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This is making my brain hurt.

jokes.text: Internet, eh? - Yes, indeedy. Making some good scratch too. - Scratch, eh? - Yep. - Maude, eh?
jokes.setup: Ned explaining his Internet business success
jokes.punchline: Homer's repetitive 'eh?' responses building to absurdity
jokes.explanation: Good escalating pattern with Homer's suspicious Canadian-esque responses. The 'Maude, eh?' caps it perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I'm mighty proud to say my Internet business is really taking off!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: I said my business is doing very well.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: My company! It's successful!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh?

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Eh? Eh?! Homer, you're doing it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh? Eh? Eh?

jokes.text: Can't discuss that now, Marge. I have to write another delicious memo. Mmm. Memo.
jokes.setup: Homer ignoring Marge's question about the butter
jokes.punchline: His sexual satisfaction with memo-writing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer absurdity - treating business memos like food. The 'Mmm. Memo.' is classic Homer voice.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, did you hear me? I asked you about the butter.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I'm writing memos. It's very satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Satisfying? More satisfying than talking to your wife?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, please. You're distracting me from my sexual satisfaction with memo-writing.

jokes.text: Man, I don't know why I bought this stupid tape.
jokes.setup: Otto playing children's songs while driving the school bus
jokes.punchline: His complaint about the tape he chose to play
jokes.explanation: Good Otto character moment showing his absent-minded nature. Simple but fits the character well.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Man, I hate this tape. All these songs are about sharing and caring.

jokes.text: Hey, Simpson. Race ya. First one to the front of the bus gets Martin's lunch money. What? Go apple! Go orange! Go banana!
jokes.setup: Nelson challenging Bart to a race for Martin's money
jokes.punchline: Kids cheering for fruit-based teams in the chaos
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of school bus chaos with absurd fruit loyalty

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: You're on, Simpson! Let's see what you got!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: You're going down, Nelson!

dialogue.speaker: Student 1
dialogue.line: Go Team Banana!

dialogue.speaker: Student 2
dialogue.line: Team Orange rules!

dialogue.speaker: Student 3
dialogue.line: Go Team Strawberry!

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: Yeah! Fruit power!

jokes.text: Make way for grapefruit. Go grapefruit!
jokes.setup: Kids racing fruit down the bus aisle
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's dramatic entrance with the largest fruit
jokes.explanation: Good escalation in the fruit racing sequence. Grapefruit as the climax fruit works well.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Behold! A watermelon!

jokes.text: Stay calm, kids. I need you to be my eyes. Okay. Which way should I turn?
jokes.setup: Otto blinded while driving a bus full of children
jokes.punchline: Asking the chaotic kids for driving directions
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of dangerous situation with poor decision-making

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Hey kids, I can't see! Can somebody give me directions?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Turn left!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, turn right!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Me fail English? That's unpossible. Also, go straight!

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: You're all driving me crazy!

jokes.text: Just hang tight, kids. I'll swim for help.
jokes.setup: Bus has crashed and is sinking
jokes.punchline: Otto abandoning the children to save himself
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy showing Otto's irresponsibility. The casual 'hang tight' while abandoning them is perfect Otto.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Every man for himself!

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: I'm outta here!

jokes.text: Zeppelin rules!
jokes.setup: Otto presumably hitting something in the water
jokes.punchline: His random rock music exclamation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Otto non-sequitur. Even in crisis, he's thinking about rock music. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Whoa!

jokes.text: I guess this is the end, Wendell. - He's Wendell. I'm Lewis. - Whatever. Just tell Wendell I said bye.
jokes.setup: Kids facing death, one trying to say goodbye
jokes.punchline: Casual confusion and dismissal even in their final moments
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy with the casual 'whatever' in a death scene. Nice deadpan delivery of the confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Lewis
dialogue.line: Goodbye, Wendell. I'm sorry we never became friends.

dialogue.speaker: Wendell
dialogue.line: We went to school together for nine years.

dialogue.speaker: Lewis
dialogue.line: I know, but we never really hung out, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Wendell
dialogue.line: We sat next to each other in homeroom.

dialogue.speaker: Lewis
dialogue.line: Yeah, but like, outside of school...

dialogue.speaker: Wendell
dialogue.line: We were on the same bus route.

dialogue.speaker: Lewis
dialogue.line: Whatever. Goodbye.

jokes.text: I think I'm getting swimmer's ear!
jokes.setup: Kids screaming in terror as they're drowning
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's mundane medical concern amid the chaos
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse neurosis - worrying about minor ailments during major crisis. Great misdirection from the terror.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: I'm getting a little nauseous.

jokes.text: Hey! Martin seconded the motion. It's entirely his fault.
jokes.setup: Bart blaming Lisa for their situation
jokes.punchline: Lisa immediately deflecting blame to Martin
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's human side - she's not above deflecting blame when pressured.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, this is all your fault!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: My fault? Bart, I didn't do anything. This is Martin's fault!

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Eh?

jokes.text: We all know this is Milhouse's fault.
jokes.setup: Chain of blame being passed around
jokes.punchline: Martin completing the circle by blaming Milhouse
jokes.explanation: Nice completion of the blame escalation. Martin's matter-of-fact delivery works well.

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Well, if you hadn't started this chain of events, none of this would have happened.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Me? I didn't do anything!

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Exactly. Your inaction is what caused all of this.

jokes.text: It'll be just like The Swiss Family Robinson, only with more cursing. We're gonna live like kings. Damn, hell, as kings.
jokes.setup: Bart's optimistic view of being stranded
jokes.punchline: His attempt to demonstrate 'more cursing' with mild profanity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart optimism and his hilariously tame version of 'more cursing.' Good character voice and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, we're stranded on this island, but hey, at least I can curse more now!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dang! Gosh! Heck!

jokes.text: How many monkey butlers will there be? One at first, but he'll train others.
jokes.setup: Bart's fantasy about island life with monkey butlers
jokes.punchline: The logical business plan for monkey butler expansion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic. The idea of a monkey butler training program is perfectly absurd yet presented matter-of-factly.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So we set up monkey butler franchises all across the island.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha! That's genius, Simpson. We'll be rolling in bananas!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and we can train them to do other stuff too — laundry, cooking, whatever.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha! Pretty soon we'll need monkey butlers to manage the monkey butlers!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, we're gonna be rich!

jokes.text: Flancrest Enterprises. D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to name his Internet company
jokes.punchline: Realizing he's using Ned's company name
jokes.explanation: Simple callback to earlier Ned reference. Homer's absent-mindedness shown through his D'oh realization.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll call it... The Leftorium!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Homer, that's my store.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, I'll call it... The Rightorium!

jokes.text: This industry moves so fast, it's really hard to tell.
jokes.setup: Marge asking what Homer's company actually does
jokes.punchline: Homer's vague non-answer that sounds like tech industry BS
jokes.explanation: Great satire of dot-com era vagueness and Homer's ability to sound business-like

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what exactly does your company do?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we're in the business of synergizing our core competencies to optimize stakeholder value through cutting-edge digital transformation and leveraging data-driven insights to pivot our go-to-market strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you work at a nuclear power plant.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, honey. That's why I have no idea what I just said.

jokes.text: How about CompuGlobalHyperMeganet?
jokes.setup: Homer asking for help with tech company name
jokes.punchline: Marge's over-the-top parody of 90s tech naming
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of late-90s tech company naming conventions. CompuGlobalHyperMeganet is perfectly ridiculous and became iconic.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I need help naming my tech company.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, just add 'e-' to the front of any word. E-pants, e-socks, e-burgers. Or use 'dot-com' at the end. Pants-dot-com, socks-dot-com, burgers-dot-com. You could also combine them: e-pants-dot-com!

jokes.text: CompuGlobalHyperMeganet. Junior vice president Homer Simpson speaking. How may I direct your call? It's Patty.
jokes.setup: Homer's overly formal business phone answer
jokes.punchline: Deflating reality that it's just Patty calling
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Homer's business pretensions. The formal greeting undercut by mundane reality.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Good afternoon. You've reached the residence of Homer J. Simpson. Please state your name and business.

dialogue.speaker: Patty
dialogue.line: Homer, it's Patty.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh... hi Patty.

jokes.text: I 'eated' the purple berries. They taste like burning.
jokes.setup: Kids finding potentially poisonous berries
jokes.punchline: Ralph's grammatically incorrect admission and bizarre taste description
jokes.explanation: Legendary Ralph Wiggum line. Perfect character voice with 'eated' grammar and the surreal 'taste like burning.' Became a cultural reference.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Mmm... poisonous.

jokes.text: Yeah? Well, your tree house looks kinda crummy too. Kinda really crummy.
jokes.setup: Defensive response to criticism about finding food
jokes.punchline: Immediate escalation from 'kinda' to 'really' crummy
jokes.explanation: Weak escalation joke without much cleverness

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Well, the food here is kinda crummy.

dialogue.speaker: Lunch Lady Doris
dialogue.line: Kinda?!

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Okay, okay... it's really crummy.

jokes.text: No food, no shelter, no monkey butlers. This island is a death hole.
jokes.setup: Listing their survival failures
jokes.punchline: Includes monkey butlers as essential survival need
jokes.explanation: Good callback that treats Bart's absurd fantasy as serious expectation

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Now class, let's review what you've learned about survival skills. Who can tell me the essentials?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Water, shelter, food, and monkey butlers.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Monkey butlers?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: You can't survive without monkey butlers.

jokes.text: Oh! Thank the good dude, I'm saved! And we can go back for the kids too. Hmm.
jokes.setup: Otto being rescued by a boat
jokes.punchline: His afterthought about rescuing the children and uncertain 'hmm'
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about Otto's priorities with perfect character-specific language ('good dude')

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Whoa, dudes, thanks for saving me!

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, we should probably save those kids too.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Hmm.

jokes.text: I think I'm gonna like it on this boat.
jokes.setup: Otto being taken away by foreign sailors
jokes.punchline: His contentment with abandoning the children
jokes.explanation: Continues the dark comedy of Otto's abandonment in character-appropriate way

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Hey man, like, totally cool! Free passage to another country? Sign me up!

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: The kids? Oh man, they'll be fine. Probably.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Duuuude, adventure awaits! *waves goodbye while being pulled onto ship*

jokes.text: I think I'm gonna like it on this boat.
jokes.setup: Otto being rescued by foreign boat
jokes.punchline: His casual acceptance of potential danger/trafficking
jokes.explanation: Good Otto character moment - his laid-back attitude even in potentially dangerous situation. Fits his oblivious nature.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Whoa, a boat! Thanks for rescuing me, dudes!

dialogue.speaker: Foreign Sailor
dialogue.line: Welcome aboard. We are heading to... a secret location.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Cool, cool. As long as there's snacks and maybe some tunes, I'm down for whatever, man.

jokes.text: Scaredy got scared!
jokes.setup: Someone running from supposed monster
jokes.punchline: Nelson's playground-level taunting
jokes.explanation: Simple but character-appropriate. Nelson's juvenile taunting fits his bully persona perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha! Running from a monster? What a baby! Ha-ha!

jokes.text: I'm so hungry I could eat at Arby's.
jokes.setup: Bart expressing extreme hunger
jokes.punchline: Using Arby's as the ultimate desperation food
jokes.explanation: Brilliant corporate joke. Using a real chain as the ultimate low point is perfect Simpsons satire. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, I'm so hungry I'd eat at Arby's.

jokes.text: I'm so hungry I could eat at Arby's. Oh, my gosh! Oh, wow! She really is hungry.
jokes.setup: Lisa expressing extreme hunger
jokes.punchline: Using willingness to eat at Arby's as the ultimate measure of desperation
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational comedy about Arby's reputation, with perfect reaction from other kids

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm so hungry, I'd eat at Arby's.

dialogue.speaker: Student 1
dialogue.line: Whoa, that's desperate!

dialogue.speaker: Student 2
dialogue.line: She must be starving!

jokes.text: I mean stomach. Gut. Crap factory.
jokes.setup: Bart correcting his use of 'tummy'
jokes.punchline: Escalating to increasingly crude but accurate terms
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Bart trying to be tough. 'Crap factory' is appropriately juvenile yet crude.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I'll stop saying 'tummy.' How about 'stomach'?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's better, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Fine. 'Gut.' 'Belly.' 'Abdomen.' 'Guts.' 'Innards.' 'Gastrointestinal tract.' 'Meat sack.'

jokes.text: Mmm! Lace: The Final Brassiere.
jokes.setup: Homer browsing the Internet
jokes.punchline: His enjoyment of a Star Trek lingerie parody
jokes.explanation: Good parody title combining Star Trek with lingerie. Fits Homer's interests and 90s Internet culture.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmmm... Star Trek lingerie parody.

jokes.text: This high-speed modem is intolerably slow.
jokes.setup: Homer experiencing 1990s internet speeds
jokes.punchline: Calling a 'high-speed' modem intolerably slow
jokes.explanation: Good period-specific tech humor about dial-up internet expectations

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! 56K modem! Now I'm on the information superhighway!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, come on... why is this taking so long? This is supposed to be high-speed!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ah, forget it. I'm going back to the regular speed highway.

jokes.text: Welcome to the Internet, my friend. How can I help you?
jokes.setup: Homer encountering the Internet King
jokes.punchline: The overly friendly, slightly sinister greeting
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Internet scam scenario. The overly helpful tone suggests something suspicious.

dialogue.speaker: Internet King
dialogue.line: Well, well, well, if it isn't Homer Simpson! Welcome, welcome to my humble domain. I've been expecting you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Expecting me? But I just got here.

dialogue.speaker: Internet King
dialogue.line: Oh, everyone comes to see the Internet King eventually. Don't be scared. I don't bite. Much.

jokes.text: Welcome to the Internet, my friend. How can I help you?
jokes.setup: Bill Gates appearing as 'The Internet King'
jokes.punchline: Personal greeting from the world's richest man
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Bill Gates subplot but not particularly funny on its own

dialogue.speaker: Bill Gates
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Bill Gates, the Internet King. I'm so rich, I own the Internet. In fact, I'm so rich, I'm going to give you a personal greeting.

jokes.text: Can I have some money now?
jokes.setup: Homer's technical jargon about Internet connections
jokes.punchline: Internet King's blunt request for money
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection. Homer's technical sophistication completely ignored for simple money grab. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, you see, it's a series of tubes...

dialogue.speaker: Internet King
dialogue.line: Just give me money.

jokes.text: Well, I guess you could have. Milhouse, I am defending you. Oh. Sorry. I'm just saying, it was either you or the monster.
jokes.setup: Lisa defending Milhouse in trial
jokes.punchline: Milhouse accidentally implicating his own defense attorney
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Milhouse's social ineptitude. His innocent honesty undermines his defense.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Your Honor, my client is innocent. The evidence is circumstantial at best.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Yeah, and Lisa told me what to say!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Milhouse, no!

dialogue.speaker: Judge Snyder
dialogue.line: So you were coached by your attorney?

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Totally! She's really good at making stuff up.

jokes.text: You liar! You did it, you lying jerk! Take that! You did it! Objection! He's not asking any questions. Hmm. I'm gonna allow this.
jokes.setup: Nelson's prosecution consisting of physical violence
jokes.punchline: Bart allowing violence instead of questions as judge
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of courtroom procedure. Bart's casual allowance of violence instead of questioning is perfect kid logic.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Nelson, you're charged with assault. How do you plead?

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Bailiff, beat him up.

jokes.text: You don't look so rich. Don't let the haircut fool you. I'm exceedingly wealthy.
jokes.setup: Homer meeting Bill Gates
jokes.punchline: Homer's defense of his wealth based on his haircut
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Homer's misplaced priorities and self-deception about his status.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bill Gates? The richest man in the world? What's your secret?

dialogue.speaker: Bill Gates
dialogue.line: Hard work, innovation, and wise investments.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm rich too. I've got this haircut. Cost me five bucks at the Kwik-E-Mart.

dialogue.speaker: Bill Gates
dialogue.line: That's... not how wealth works.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Are you saying my haircut isn't worth billions?

jokes.text: Don't let the haircut fool you. I'm exceedingly wealthy. Get a load of the bowl job, Marge.
jokes.setup: Bill Gates commenting on not looking rich
jokes.punchline: Homer's oblivious insult about Gates' haircut to Marge
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy with Homer's social obliviousness toward powerful person

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Marge, look at this guy. Bill Gates, richest man in the world, and he can't even afford a decent haircut.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's Bill Gates.

jokes.text: We're rich! Richer than astronauts!
jokes.setup: Homer thinking he's sold his business successfully
jokes.punchline: His bizarre metric for extreme wealth
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic. 'Richer than astronauts' is perfectly absurd - astronauts aren't particularly wealthy but sound impressive.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm finally rich! I sold my business for three million dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know what this means? I can finally afford the really expensive stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Like... a solid gold toilet seat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No wait, even better - I can hire someone to follow me around all day and laugh at my jokes!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm talking about REAL wealth here - being able to buy as many donuts as I want without having to choose between sprinkles or jelly filling!

jokes.text: Well, everyone always does. Buy him out, boys.
jokes.setup: Homer accepting the buyout deal
jokes.punchline: Gates revealing his hostile takeover methods
jokes.explanation: Great dark turn satirizing Microsoft's business practices with perfect delivery

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: So you'll give me fifty million dollars for my internet company?

dialogue.speaker: Bill Gates
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm going to destroy your company, ruin your life, and buy your house at a foreclosure auction for twelve dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Oh, I didn't get rich by writing a lot of checks.
jokes.setup: Gates seemingly buying Homer's company
jokes.punchline: His explanation while his men destroy Homer's office
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy presenting Gates as a ruthless businessman. The line works on multiple levels - literally and metaphorically.

dialogue.speaker: Bill Gates
dialogue.line: I'm buying your company for its potential. We're going to streamline operations, cut unnecessary expenses, and maximize shareholder value.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, really? So I keep my job?

dialogue.speaker: Bill Gates
dialogue.line: Of course. We're eliminating middle management. You're middle management.

jokes.text: Here, Milhouse. You go first. Okay. Now throw the vine back. There's no time.
jokes.setup: Bart helping Milhouse escape across a chasm
jokes.punchline: Immediately abandoning him once safely across
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about abandoning friends, typical of kid survival logic

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Come on, Milhouse! You can make it!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: I'm trying, Bart!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Almost there, buddy!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: I made it! I'm safe!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: See you, loser!

jokes.text: Stop! You are in violation of the Model U.N. charter! Uh, that's right. The U.N. doesn't look too kindly on-
jokes.setup: Lisa invoking UN authority to stop violence
jokes.punchline: Bart's attempted backup being interrupted by real monster
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to earlier UN satire, interrupted by plot development

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: According to UN Resolution 4715, you must cease this violence immediately!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I'm calling my friend Godzilla!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did somebody say Godzilla?

jokes.text: Hey. If a boar can survive here, there must be a source of food. Hey. I only stole two sandwiches and a bag of Doritos.
jokes.setup: Lisa realizing the boar's survival means available food
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's casual admission of his actual theft
jokes.explanation: Good revelation that Milhouse did steal some food, but far less than accused. Nice resolution to the conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait, if the boar survived, that means there's fresh meat available for the whole town.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Yeah, about that... I didn't actually hunt anything. I just stole a roast from the grocery store.

jokes.text: All that slime made this boar extra tender. More snout anyone?
jokes.setup: Kids eating the boar that was their 'monster'
jokes.punchline: Casual offer of the least appetizing part
jokes.explanation: Decent dark comedy about eating their former tormentor

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey, who wants the bladder?

jokes.text: Ah, shut up. Savages.
jokes.setup: Kids enjoying their cooked boar feast
jokes.punchline: Lisa's disgusted reaction while presumably eating it too
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's moral compromise. Her disgust while participating shows internal conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Ugh, I can't believe I'm eating a boar. This is disgusting.

jokes.text: And eventually, they were rescued by... oh, let's say Moe.
jokes.setup: Narrator wrapping up the story
jokes.punchline: Casual, arbitrary assignment of the rescue to Moe
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about lazy storytelling. 'Let's say Moe' perfectly captures arbitrary plot resolution. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: And so, the town of Springfield was saved by the quick thinking and bravery of one man... Moe Szyslak.

tmdb_overview: When Otto drives the school bus off a bridge, the kids wind up on a deserted island, fending for themselves in a “Lord of the Flies” parody. Back in Springfield, Homer takes on Bill Gates.
insight: School bus cliff-hanger sustains 67 jokes across 22 minutes with dark survival comedy.
